title: Cooking Grease / Oil on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; powder + Dawn × 2 (oleophilic), no hot water / hot dryer
stainChemistry: Cooking grease and cooking oil are non-polar lipids that don't mix with water. On polyester, two complications stack: polyester is OLEOPHILIC and grabs oil tight, AND polyester is heat-sensitive — hot water sets the oil into the fiber and yellows the polyester over time. The yellowing of polyester collars and cuffs is exactly this combination playing out over years of hot-washed and hot-dried oil residue. The recurring traps: hot water (sets oil + yellows polyester), hot dryer (locks oil + permanent yellow), water-first instead of powder-first (spreads the oil into a wider ring), and skipping the second Dawn pass (polyester typically needs 2 passes to fully release the oil).
whyThisWorks: Powder preload pulls bulk oil up before water touches it. Generous Dawn pretreat — Dawn is engineered for cooking grease — emulsifies the oil polyester grabbed onto. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes because of oleophilic affinity. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap: aged hot-dried oil yellowing on polyester is often a permanent shadow because polyester yellows under heat once oil is in the fiber.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): cooking grease and oil are triglyceride lipids; polyester is hydrophobic and oleophilic. Oxidized oil polymerizes under heat. Pro uses POG / petroleum ether for oil + amyl acetate for polymerized residue + NSD. Sodium hydrosulfite at controlled dilution for set yellow on white polyester.
defaultAssumption: Cooking spatter on a polyester shirt or kitchen apron. Fresh = real shot at full removal. Aged hot-dried yellowing = honest cap; manage expectations. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester = pro from minute one.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull bulk oil up before water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot — Dawn is engineered for cooking grease. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.agent: Test Oily Feel — Repeat Dawn If Yes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Touch with a clean fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for cooking oil.
Cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks future cleaning. NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester.
AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow = repeat Dawn. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — locks it into polyester and yellows fiber over time.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains (2 passes typical on polyester).
5. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect. Never heat until clear.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets oil into polyester and yellows over time
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks future cleaning
Aged hot-dried yellowing on polyester is often permanent

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester Loves Cooking Oil — Powder First, Dawn Twice
homeSolutions.instruction: Cooking oil and polyester are a tough combination because polyester is OLEOPHILIC (grabs oil tight) and heat-sensitive (hot water sets the oil and yellows the polyester). The path: powder preload → Dawn → repeat Dawn → cool wash → air-dry. Three hard rules: powder BEFORE water (water-first spreads oil into a wider ring), NO hot water (sets oil + yellows polyester), and NO hot dryer until cleared (locks the residue forever).

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda — 20 to 30 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the oil spot with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain baby powder. Press gently. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off.

Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Dawn is engineered for cooking grease. Polyester needs longer dwell than cotton because it grabs oil tight. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Test for Oily Feel — Repeat If Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch the spot with a clean fingertip. Still feels oily? Repeat the Dawn step. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for cooking oil.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool Wash, No Fabric Softener, AIR-DRY
homeSolutions.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener — it coats the fiber and makes any future buildup harder to clean. AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow shadow = repeat the Dawn pass. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — heat polymerizes the oil into polyester and the yellow shadow becomes permanent.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared. NO fabric softener.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Repeat Dawn pass if oily feel remains (2 passes typical).
6. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect. No dryer until cleared.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent yellow shadow on polyester). Stop on performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 3 home cycles plateau.
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot water — sets oil + yellows polyester
NEVER fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks cleaning
Polyester is oleophilic — 2 Dawn passes typical
Aged hot-dried yellowing is often permanent

neverDo: Never water-first on oil stains — spreads them
Never hot water on oil + polyester — sets it permanently
Never chlorine bleach on polyester — damages fiber, yellows long-term
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — grabs cooking oil tightly, needs 2 Dawn passes
Polyester yellows under heat over time when oil is present
Performance / specialty polyester (compression, technical): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Athletic wear with fabric softener buildup: strip softener first or expect plateau

materialWarnings: Powder before water — water-first spreads oil
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester yellows under heat with oil
Polyester loves cooking oil — Dawn × 2 typical
No fabric softener — coats fiber, blocks future cleaning
Aged hot-dried yellowing on polyester is often permanent

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent yellow on polyester). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. 3 home cycles plateau. Aged yellowing across collar / cuff / underarm region (cumulative buildup).
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking grease / oil on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected / years buildup], home-treated with [cornstarch preload + Dawn × 2 + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no fabric softener. [Residual yellow shadow / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability for set yellow on polyester

contextQuestions: Cooking oil (vegetable / olive / canola), animal fat, butter, or rendered grease?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Fresh, hours old, or weeks/months of hot-dried buildup?
Has fabric softener been used on past washes?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Polyester's oleophilic + heat-sensitive combination is exactly why polyester collars and cuffs gradually yellow — it's cumulative cooking-oil + body-oil residue locked in by years of hot-drying. Manage expectations on aged yellowing. Athletic-wear fabric softener buildup is the hidden plateau cause; strip the softener (white vinegar pre-wash) before treatment if past washes used softener.
